reproducible_artifact: Per-engineer attention-surface audit: a written list of every input channel that fires during deep-work blocks, classified by signal-vs-noise, with restraint policy per channel (mute-during-block, batch-to-digest, allow-always-but-fire-rate-limited, allow-always). Reviewed at start of each deep-work block.
embodiment_sketch: An engineer maintains a `attention-surfaces.md` document in her dotfiles repo. Sections: (1) Channel inventory — Slack (per-channel config), PagerDuty, Linear, GitHub mentions, Sentry alerts, Twitter, news sites; (2) Per-channel SNR estimate based on a 30-day audit (e.g., 'Slack #payments-team: SNR 4/10 — most messages do not require my action'); (3) Restraint policy per channel during a deep-work block — Slack channel-specific DND, GitHub mentions batched into a 12:00/17:00 digest email, Twitter blocked at DNS layer for the work window, PagerDuty-only for actual production-severity my-services. (4) A pre-block ritual: 'enter deep work' shell command that toggles all the configured restraints simultaneously. (5) An exit ritual that restores. Falsification test: across 4 weeks of A/B (pre-block ritual vs. no-ritual blocks of equal scheduled length on similar work), measure the post-block reviewer-discovered defect rate. The verse predicts that ritual-on blocks produce a lower reviewer-discovered defect rate. If they do not, the wind was not the input stream — it was something else (caffeine, sleep debt, the work itself being too ambiguous). Pseudo-code: `function deepWorkBlock(start, end) { saveState(); applyRestraints(restraintPolicy.deepWork); doWork(); restoreState(); auditPostBlock(); }`. The verse's claim is encoded as: the SNR-classified attention-surface restraint is auditable, and the audit is the falsification test.
